mnemonics.title: RST for Equivalence
mnemonics.content: R-S-T: Reflexive-Symmetric-Transitive = Equivalence Relation. Remember: Equal Relations 'EQ' = R + S + T

mnemonics.title: RAT for Partial Order
mnemonics.content: RAT: Reflexive-Antisymmetric-Transitive = Partial Order. Think: 'RAT' bites (antisymmetric)!'

mnemonics.title: Domain-Range Memory
mnemonics.content: Domain: Left-first elements (input). Range: Right-second elements (output). DR = D↔R (Domain is Left, Range is Right)

acronyms.usage: Three properties that define an Equivalence Relation
Three properties that define a Partial Order Relation
Set of all first coordinates in ordered pairs
Set of all second coordinates in ordered pairs

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a grid/table. Rows represent set A, columns represent set B. Each cell contains one ordered pair (a,b)
Draw two ovals for sets A and B. Draw arrows from a in A to b in B if (a,b) ∈ R. This is a directed graph representation.
Square matrix for relations on same set. Row = from element, Column = to element. 1 at (i,j) if (aᵢ,aⱼ) ∈ R.
Draw nodes for elements. Draw upward edges for covering relation. Omit transitive edges and self-loops.
Partition the set into disjoint circles. Elements in same circle are related to each other.

analogies.analogy: Like a train schedule: Origin cities (set A) × Destination cities (set B) gives all possible routes (ordered pairs)
Like a friendship list between two groups. Person from group A 'knows' person from group B.
Everyone loves themselves (self-love). Each person is related to themselves.
If A loves B, then B loves A (like mutual friendship). Goes both ways.
If A > B and B > C, then A > C. Like a chain reaction.
Family tree hierarchy. An ancestor is 'above' descendants. You cannot be your own ancestor (antisymmetric).
